source.title: Chemical Reaction, Decomposition, and Toxic Gas Release at Bio-Lab, Inc.

event.incident_type: chemical reaction, decomposition, and toxic gas release
event.costs: $250 million facility reconstruction; approximately $10 billion insured damages in Louisiana from Hurricane Laura
actions_taken: Bio-Lab employees obtained SCBAs and attempted to assess the situation
Employees attempted to start rental backup generator to power electric fire water pumps
One diesel fire water pump was manually started
Employees identified and stopped a water leak at adjacent Lonza facility
Lake Charles Fire Department arrived and attempted to draft water from Bio-Lab freshwater reservoir
Third-party responders (US Fire Pump, SRS, Haz Mat Special Services) contracted and deployed
Emergency responders demolished Plant 4 Low Bay west wall and roof to access decomposing chemicals
Copious amounts of water applied to saturate decomposing TCCA-based formulation
Calcasieu Parish issued shelter-in-place order for nearby community
Louisiana State Police closed portion of Interstate 10 for over 28 hours
LDEQ and EPA conducted air monitoring
Finished Goods Warehouse back wall removed to spray water on decomposing super sacks
Decomposing super sacks removed from Finished Goods Warehouse and saturated with water
Bio-Lab invested approximately $250 million to reconstruct the damaged facility with enhanced safety features
event.summary: On August 27, 2020, Category 4 Hurricane Laura winds damaged the roofs of Bio-Lab Plant 4 and the Finished Goods Warehouse in Westlake, Louisiana. Rainwater contacted stored TCCA-based formulation, initiating a chemical reaction and decomposition that released a large plume of toxic chlorine gas and caused a fire. The incident destroyed Plant 4 and damaged additional structures. Emergency response was severely delayed (~5.5 hours) due to an inadequate and largely nonfunctional fire protection system, inability to operate the rental backup generator, and absence of automated sprinkler systems. A shelter-in-place order was issued and Interstate 10 was closed for over 28 hours. There were no reported injuries. Contributing factors included Bio-Lab's inadequate extreme weather preparation, deficient PHA action item management, absence of automated fire suppression, and insufficient regulatory coverage of reactive chemical hazards.
recommendations: 2020-05-I-LA-R1: Bio-Lab to evaluate hurricane hazards and implement safeguards including building to current wind design requirements and following CCPS Monograph on Natural Hazards
2020-05-I-LA-R2: Bio-Lab to develop and implement improved PHA action item management system with assignment, documentation, and tracking requirements
2020-05-I-LA-R3: Bio-Lab to perform PHAs on all TCCA processing/storage buildings, revalidated every five years, including building design basis as process safety information
2020-05-I-LA-R4: Bio-Lab to revise emergency response plan to require proper fire protection system maintenance, pre-hurricane equipment checks, and personnel training on emergency generators
2020-05-I-LA-R5: Louisiana Governor/Legislature/LDEQ to require existing chemical facilities in hurricane-prone regions to evaluate and protect against hurricane hazards
2020-05-I-LA-R6: EPA to implement five open GAO recommendations on climate change risks at RMP facilities
2001-01-H-R1 (reiterated): OSHA to amend PSM Standard to achieve more comprehensive control of reactive hazards
2001-01-H-R3 (reiterated): EPA to revise Accidental Release Prevention Requirements to explicitly cover catastrophic reactive hazards
key_phrases: CCPS Monograph Assessment of and Planning for Natural Hazards

hazards.name: Large inventory of water-reactive TCCA-based formulation stored in buildings susceptible to hurricane wind damage
Hurricane-force winds and associated rainfall in a hurricane-prone region

threats.name: Hurricane Laura winds damaging building roofs, allowing rainwater intrusion into TCCA storage areas
Buildings not constructed to current wind design requirements (built to 1974 Standard Building Code, not 2015 IBC)
Inadequate extreme weather preparation and failure to implement CCPS natural hazard guidance
Unimplemented 2010 PHA recommendation to evaluate warehouse roof structure for hurricane conditions

consequences.name: Release of large toxic chlorine gas plume into surrounding community
Fire and destruction of Plant 4 production building and damage to additional structures
Shelter-in-place order issued for nearby community
Increased quantity of TCCA decomposed and chlorine released due to ~5.5-hour emergency response delay

controls.name: Hurricane Plan (pre-hurricane preparation procedures)
controls.barrier_role: Procedural controls to protect chemicals from hurricane rainwater and floodwater
controls.lod_basis: Bio-Lab Hurricane Plan defining six preparation phases including sandbagging, raising materials on pallets, securing warehouse doors

supporting_text: To protect chemicals from hurricane rainwater and floodwater, the plan requires employees to 'sandbag identified entrances to prevent floodwater entry,' 'move finished product… to higher storage or place empty pallet under the product'
These actions could not protect chemicals from any unintentional movement from hurricane winds or exposure to rainwater in the event hurricane-force winds damaged the buildings.

Bio-Lab did not learn the importance of preparing for extreme weather after the 2017 Arkema incident, did not adequately implement industry guidance for preparing for extreme weather, and, as a result, was unprepared for the winds produced by Category 4 Hurricane Laura.

controls.name: Building structural integrity (wind-resistant construction)
controls.barrier_role: Physical barrier preventing rainwater intrusion into chemical storage areas during hurricane
controls.lod_basis: Buildings designed to 1974 Standard Building Code wind requirements; not meeting 2015 IBC Risk Category IV requirements of 145 mph

supporting_text: winds from Hurricane Laura caused portions of the roof of Bio-Lab Plant 4, which housed an estimated 70,000-100,000 pounds of TCCA-based formulation material, to be torn off the building

The CSB-commissioned analysis determined that buildings meeting 2015 International Building Code requirements are significantly stronger and better able to survive high wind events than those meeting the 1974 Standard Building Code requirements.

the cause of the accidental release of chlorine gas from the Bio-Lab Lake Charles facility was rainwater contacting stored trichloroisocyanuric acid-based formulation, which initiated a chemical reaction, decomposition, and fire after Category 4 Hurricane Laura winds damaged portions of the facility's building roofs that were not built to current wind design requirements.

controls.name: Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) program
controls.barrier_role: Systematic identification of hazards and implementation of corrective actions to prevent chemical releases
controls.lod_basis: Bio-Lab voluntarily conducted PHAs; 2010 PHA identified warehouse roof hurricane vulnerability but recommendation was not implemented

supporting_text: Bio-Lab's 2010 Process Hazard Analysis recommended the Lake Charles facility to 'consider evaluating warehouse roof structure for hurricane conditions; verify warehouse is built to withstand high winds,' but the company did not implement the recommendation.
Bio-Lab has no documentation that this 2010 PHA recommendation was ever implemented.
The lack of close-out documentation for the 2010 PHA recommendation points to deficiencies in Bio-Lab's PHA action item management system.
Bio-Lab communicated that it does not have a PHA action item management system.

controls.name: Pre-hurricane chemical removal/transport from facility
controls.barrier_role: Reduce on-site inventory of water-reactive TCCA to limit exposure and consequence magnitude
controls.lod_basis: Bio-Lab transported approximately 825,000 pounds of TCCA-based formulation to Conyers, Georgia before hurricane landfall; two additional scheduled trucks never arrived

supporting_text: Bio-Lab successfully transported about 825,000 pounds of TCCA-based formulation from the Finished Goods Warehouse to the Bio-Lab facility in Conyers, Georgia. However, two additional scheduled trucks never arrived at the facility, leaving over one million pounds of TCCA-based formulation product on-site as the hurricane approached.

controls.name: Fire protection system (fire water pumps, monitors, hydrants)
controls.barrier_role: Apply water to decomposing TCCA to suppress chemical reaction, fire, and toxic gas release
controls.lod_basis: Four fire water pumps (two electric, two diesel) with total capacity of 8,000 gpm; fire monitors, hose stations, hydrants

supporting_text: Bio-Lab experienced serious delays in responding to the TCCA-based formulation decomposition and fire due to an inadequate and largely nonfunctional fire protection system and the absence of automated sprinkler systems.
one of the diesel pumps was nonfunctional before the hurricane made landfall, resulting in only one fire water pump—a diesel pump—available for use

The diesel fire water pump that was not functional at the time of the incident had failed its annual performance test in 2015, 2016, 2017, and 2020. A Bio-Lab manager communicated to the CSB that the pump 'didn't work and it hasn't for some years.'
With only one fire water pump, the facility could not achieve adequate pressure to apply water to the decomposing TCCA-based formulation.
The problems with the Bio-Lab fire protection system resulted in a lengthy delay of around five and a half hours before productive emergency response operations began.

controls.name: Backup generator for electric fire water pumps
controls.barrier_role: Provide electrical power to operate electric fire water pumps during power outage
controls.lod_basis: Rental backup generator acquired during hurricane preparation; Bio-Lab's own backup generator was not functional before the hurricane

supporting_text: One of the employees attempted to turn on a rental backup generator secured during the site's preparation for Hurricane Laura to energize the large electric fire water pump. The employee, who was unfamiliar with the rental generator, was unable to start it.
Bio-Lab's backup emergency generator was not functional.
The employees did not have a manual or operating procedure to reference for the rental generator.

Bio-Lab also did not ensure that enough of its staff, including its assigned hurricane crew, knew how to activate the rental backup generator, which was necessary to operate electrically powered fire protection equipment during the power outage caused by Hurricane Laura.

controls.name: Automatic fire sprinkler system
controls.barrier_role: Automatically apply water to suppress fire and chemical decomposition upon detection
controls.lod_basis: Required by NFPA 101 for high-hazard industrial occupancies and NFPA 400 for facilities exceeding MAQ; not installed in Plant 4 or Finished Goods Warehouse

supporting_text: Bio-Lab did not have automated fire sprinklers in Plant 4 or the Finished Goods Warehouse, which stored TCCA-based formulation and experienced decomposition events due to the hurricane.
Bio-Lab also did not conform to NFPA 400 Hazardous Materials Code, which required a fire detection system and an automatic fire sprinkler system.

Had the Bio-Lab buildings involved in the incident been equipped with automatic extinguishing systems, it is likely that Bio-Lab would have begun applying large amounts of water to the decomposing TCCA-based formulation earlier in the incident.

The approximately five-and-a-half hour delay in responding to the decomposition likely led to an unnecessary increase in (1) the amount of TCCA-based formulation that decomposed, (2) the quantity of toxic chlorine released, and (3) the extent of the facility damage.

controls.name: Fire detection system
controls.barrier_role: Detect fire or decomposition event early to trigger timely emergency response
controls.lod_basis: Required by NFPA 400 for facilities storing hazardous materials exceeding MAQ; not installed in Plant 4 or Finished Goods Warehouse

supporting_text: Bio-Lab had neither a fire detection system nor an automatic fire sprinkler system in either Plant 4 or the Finished Goods Warehouse, where the decompositions occurred.
NFPA 400 Hazardous Materials Code, which required a fire detection system and an automatic fire sprinkler system

controls.name: Emergency response plan and trained hurricane crew
controls.barrier_role: Coordinate and execute emergency response to chemical decomposition and fire events
controls.lod_basis: Bio-Lab Hurricane Plan and designated Hurricane Crew; crew members had specific job assignments but lacked training on rental generator operation

supporting_text: Bio-Lab did not ensure that enough of its staff, including its assigned hurricane crew, knew how to activate the rental backup generator, which was necessary to operate electrically powered fire protection equipment during the power outage caused by Hurricane Laura.
The employees did not have a manual or operating procedure to reference for the rental generator.

Bio-Lab experienced serious delays in responding to the TCCA-based formulation decomposition and fire (roughly five and a half hours), due to an inadequate and largely nonfunctional fire protection system and the absence of automated extinguishing systems.

controls.name: Third-party emergency responders (US Fire Pump, SRS, Haz Mat Special Services)
controls.barrier_role: Provide firefighting equipment, water application, and hazmat response to control decomposition and release
controls.lod_basis: External contractors and fire department deployed after Bio-Lab's internal response failed; US Fire Pump flowing fire water by approximately 3:21 p.m.

supporting_text: By approximately 3:21 p.m., US Fire Pump was on-site and flowing fire water to Plant 4 with its firefighting equipment.

The incident could not be controlled until the contracted third-party emergency responders arrived, set up their equipment, and successfully flowed sufficient water to the decomposing TCCA-based formulation.
Once Haz Mat Special Services demolished the wall and roof, they accessed the chemical and saturated it with water, significantly reducing the visible white cloud emitting from the building.

controls.name: Air monitoring program (LDEQ, EPA, Center for Toxicology & Environmental Health)
controls.barrier_role: Monitor community and work area chlorine concentrations to inform protective actions
controls.lod_basis: LDEQ arrived ~12:05 p.m.; EPA ASPECT aircraft at ~2:00 p.m.; ground monitoring at 41+ locations; chlorine measured at up to 0.9 ppm in community

supporting_text: The Louisiana Department of Environmental Quality (LDEQ) arrived at the facility around 12:05 p.m. on August 27, 2020, and began coordinating air monitoring efforts.
The EPA conducted handheld air monitoring for chlorine gas in multiple ground locations.

The EPA measured chlorine gas 'slightly' exceeding the EPA Acute Exposure Guideline Level (AEGL-1) of 0.5 ppm in two locations on August 27, 2020: 0.9 ppm of chlorine measured at 5:38 p.m., and 0.7 ppm of chlorine measured at 9:54 p.m.

controls.name: Shelter-in-place order and Interstate 10 closure
controls.barrier_role: Protect community members from toxic chlorine gas exposure
controls.lod_basis: Shelter-in-place issued at ~10:15 a.m.; I-10 closed at 9:54 a.m.; both lifted after incident controlled on August 28

supporting_text: At 9:54 a.m., the Louisiana State Police closed a portion of the nearby Interstate 10.
At about 10:15 a.m., a shelter-in-place order was issued for the nearby community.

On August 28, 2020, at approximately 2:30 p.m., the incident was sufficiently controlled, and the Louisiana State Police reopened Interstate 10 after it had been closed for 28 hours and lifted the shelter-in-place order.
There were no reported injuries from this event.

controls.name: OSHA PSM Standard and EPA RMP Rule regulatory coverage
controls.barrier_role: Require facilities to implement process safety management elements including PHAs and action item resolution for reactive chemical hazards
controls.lod_basis: TCCA and TCCA-based formulations not covered by OSHA PSM or EPA RMP regulations; regulatory gap contributed to incident

supporting_text: TCCA and TCCA-based formulations are not regulated under the OSHA PSM Standard or the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Risk Management Program (RMP) Rule.
As such, Bio-Lab was not required to implement baseline process safety management system elements to manage the safety of its TCCA-based formulation related operations under these regulations.
Also contributing to the incident was insufficient regulatory coverage of chemicals with reactive hazards.

This regulatory coverage gap relating to reactive chemicals and their hazards (1) points to a weakness in relying on fixed chemical lists to determine regulatory coverage, (2) contributed to this incident, and (3) contributed to many other reactive chemical incidents over the past three decades.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
